passRule.note: Pass = 100% on safety questions AND 90% overall.

syllabus: The two priorities & the PRACE values
The customer is #1: Respect, Help, Interact
Helmets, belts & the two-finger test
Kart operation: charging, pit gate, on/off, sending karts
The safety briefing
Getting in safely; always walk on the yellow
Pass:  100% on safety questions, 90% overall.

modules.title: The Two Priorities
content: BLUNT · FUNNY WITHOUT TRYING · NEVER PRECIOUS
THE POWERPLAY TEAM PLAYBOOK — DELIVERING THE TRACK EXPERIENCE
We're here to help.

Not to shout "brake." Our job is to help every customer go as fast as they want — safely. Everyone in the venue needs to know how the experience is delivered: just because you're on the front desk doesn't mean you stop paying attention to the track.
YOUR TWO PRIORITIES
KEEP PEOPLE SAFE
DELIVER THE EXPERIENCE
WE DON'T SELL GO-KARTING.
WE SELL THE FEELING PEOPLE
HAVE WHEN THEY GET OUT.
Deliver that and we're PowerPlay. Don't, and we're just another go-kart venue. Educate well and the race runs itself — bigger smiles. You are the product.

GMac built PowerPlay to share a little of what he felt across 30+ years competing in rallies all over the world. Every detail matters. No shortcuts. Complete focus on delivering the experience. EDUCATION — AND HELPING PEOPLE ENJOY THE EXPERIENCE — IS THE KEY. BIGGER SMILES ARE THE REWARD.
practicalCriteria: States the two priorities unprompted: keep people safe, deliver the experience
Explains “we sell the feeling, not go-karting” in their own words
Gives a real example of putting safety first when priorities compete

modules.title: PowerPlay On A Page
content: ONE PAGE.
THE WHOLE JOB.
POWERPLAY ON A PAGE
QUICK REFERENCE — IF YOU READ ONE PAGE, READ THIS
THE TWO PRIORITIES
1.  Keep people safe.
Deliver the experience.
Safety first – Keep everyone safe, both the customers and the team. We have the best of safety equipment and standards yet we rely on you to always have safety as your first thought.
THE CUSTOMER IS #1
Respect · Help · Interact.
Talking to people is your whole job.

Never tell them off — there’s no “DON’T” on any sign at PowerPlay. You need to think like this too. Always seek to help people. Interact with customers at any opportunity talk with them about their experience.
ON TRACK — LESS IS MORE
Read the play. Signal first.
Call “brake” only if you don’t see them braking.
Watch their feet down the ramp on the brake pedal – if you don’t seem them brake when you think they should the react instantly – hand signals and ”brake”.
HAND SIGNALS
Point = look here  ·  Hand up = brake
Pull back = ease off  ·  Point the line
Look up  ·  Turn in  ·  Thumbs up = nailed it
The PSL – PowerPlay Sign Language is the key to connecting with each customer on track. Together with your voice you will cut through the noise and help them.
RECOVERY & STOPPAGES
Stand on the safe side. Speed 1 if needed.
Three chances, then off the track to coach.
Keep the race flowing.

No one likes it when we slow the karts down. We should minimize how much we do this every race. That means great briefings, good education and pulling people over that need more help. Always try to keep the race moving.
THE DE-BRIEF – SUPER IMPORTANT
Result  ·  genuine praise  ·  one tip.
Create the bonded moment – real connection.
It’s why they come back.

Interacting with the customers post race is a lot more than just helping to take their helmet off. Ask them how their race was, where did they finish, which corner they liked best, which was the biggest challenge?
THE ACADEMY:  Trainee → Rookie → Rally → WRC → Champion → ★ GMac Standard
If you read one page, read this.  ·  2 / 51
practicalCriteria: Walks through the one-page overview confidently
Uses it to answer a “what do I do when…” question

modules.title: Our Values — PRACE
content: WHO WE ARE
OUR VALUES
PRACE.
The five values behind everything in this playbook and the detail that PowerPlay is built on.
Gmac wants us to live them on every shift.
P
PASSIONATE
“I am Passionate”
My enthusiasm demonstrates it.
R
RESPECTFUL
“I am Respectful”
Taking responsibility for my actions and attitude.
A
ACCOUNTABLE
“I am Accountable”
I know my role, proactively implement it, and seek help when needed.
C
COMMITTED
“I am Committed”
To teamwork and the PowerPlay community.
E
EMPOWERING
“I Empower”
Myself and others through education.
Who we are — live it every shift.
The safety and experience of our guests and team is our number one priority. We strive for 100% safety, 100% of the time — every decision is guided by it.
practicalCriteria: Names all five PRACE values
Gives a real on-floor example for each value
Demonstrates a value in a live interaction

modules.title: Who We Are
content: FROM THE BRAND BIBLE
WHO WE ARE
What · How · Why.
Not just go-karting. PowerPlay is a next-level immersive experience — here's what that means.
WHAT DO WE DO?
We provide an epic, immersive racing experience on our unique two-level tracks.
They are slippery by design, so they challenge even the seasoned racer.
This is a rally inspired go karting experience.
HOW DO WE DO IT?
We inspire growth and confidence through intense, playful and measurable competition.
We teach people to go as fast as they want to go
The scoreboard is huge for a reason, everything at PowerPlay is a competition.
WHY DO WE DO IT?
After spending a lifetime rallying around the workd, Gmac’s aim was, and is to create an environment where people can experience a little of what he felt.
Where people can come together make memories and 'bonded moments', experiencing things they never thought possible.
CORE FOCUS — “BONDED MOMENTS”

To deliver a safe, fun, clean, immersive, empowering and competitive racing experience that makes people feel happy, positive and connected to each other. We call this creating “Bonded Moments”. Real People + Real Smiles + Real Connection = Bonded Moments.
Not just go-karting.
practicalCriteria: Explains what we do, how we do it, and why
Describes a “bonded moment” and how to create one

modules.title: The Customer Is Number 1
content: RESPECT THEM.
NEVER TELL THEM OFF.
THE CUSTOMER IS NUMBER 1
EVERY STEP IS ABOUT THE PERSON IN FRONT OF YOU
Everything in this playbook serves one thing — the customer. We keep them safe because we respect them, we help them have a great time by educating them and then we connect.
RESPECT
Treat every customer with respect. Never tell them off — no negative language, no “DON’T” – not unless they are compromising someone else’s safety then ask them to respect others.
They came to have fun, not to be corrected. Coach not correct.
HELP

Help them first. Show them, guide them, make it easy —  the welcome, the guidance into the driver zone, the chat, the briefing, the helmet, the strapping into the go kart, the help on track, the post race into pit lane, the debrief, the results and the podium.
Talk to them, guide them, make them feel special. Coach on track,
INTERACT
Then interact: chat, encourage, celebrate.
People are the experience — the connection is what they take home.
The pre-race interaction tells you who they are, if they are super confident or nervous and everything in between.

AT EVERY STEP, ASK: am I respecting them, helping them, connecting with them? It matters most when someone refuses a helmet, when a race keeps stopping, and in the de-brief — help them, never tell them off.
Respect. Help. Interact.  ·  5 / 51
practicalCriteria: Demonstrates Respect, Help and Interact in a live or role-played interaction
Handles a difficult guest politely — never tells a customer off
Puts the customer first when priorities compete

modules.title: Greet and Read The Room
content: TALKING TO PEOPLE
IS YOUR WHOLE JOB.
CUSTOMER INTERACTION
PRE-RACE — GREET, READ THE ROOM, BUILD RAPPORT
The customer is the undisputed number 1. It starts before they’re in the kart — read each person and talk to them.
GREET & BUILD RAPPORT
“Welcome to PowerPlay! Been here before?”
“Date night? Are you all here together?”
“Special occasion — what brings you in?”
”how many different groups do we have here?”
Be friendly and respectful — this is your whole job.
Read who wants to chat and who doesn’t.
READ THE ROOM
Nervous? → they need more help and reassurance. They may not ask questions so look to help.
Own helmet? → they’ve raced before.
Own gloves? → very keen, maybe overconfident — watch them.
Match the group’s energy.
Special Needs Group – gauge the level of ability
Kids Birthday Party – how many have never been go kartng?
KEEP THEM SAFE — KINDLY
“Helmet feels tight? It still comes off — and tight is what keeps you safe.”
Always walk on the yellow path in the Pit Lane.
Getting in: one leg next to the steering wheel, then swing the other leg over — seat yes, plastic no.
“We’ll come round and do everyone’s belts up.”
Switch off all go karts before they get out of the go karts.
BUILD THEM UP & READ CUES
“Ready to have some fun? Who’s winning today?”
Own helmet? “Have you done much racing?”
Listen for experience — it tells you how to run their race.
You’re always looking for cues.
Been here before – check past history.
Talking to people is your whole job.  ·  6 / 51
practicalCriteria: Greets guests warmly on arrival
Reads the group type and adjusts their approach
Makes guests feel looked after before they’re in the kart

modules.title: The PowerPlay Academy
content: KNOW IT.
CLIMB IT.
THE POWERPLAY ACADEMY
OUR RANKING & CERTIFICATION SYSTEM
Everyone climbs the same ladder. You qualify by proving you know the playbook and can deliver it — knowledge and the experience move you up. Reading this is how you start the climb.
TRAINEE   ·   IN TRAINING
Learns safety and the customer-first basics — helps and watches, supervised.
ROOKIE   ·   RUNS RACES
Runs a race solo: the briefing, strapping in, coaching with signals, the de-brief.
RALLY   ·   TRACK LEAD
Leads the floor and coaches the coaches. Speed 4 qualified — sets the example.
WRC   ·   TRACK MANAGER
Owns the track, the karts, standards, incidents and the Education Score day-to-day.
DRIVER COACH   ·   SENIOR LEADERS · MARK & COOPER
Sets the standard across the team and venues; develops Track Managers.
THE GMAC STANDARD   ·   DAYMON & GMAC
The gold standard — bonded moments every time. What we’re all measured against.
Know it. Climb it.  ·  7 / 51
practicalCriteria: Explains the ladder: Trainee → Rookie → Rally → WRC → Champion
Describes how they move up and what’s expected

modules.title: Certification Explained
content: ONLINE + PRACTICAL.
THEN SIGNED OFF.
CERTIFICATION
EVERY LEVEL — ONLINE TEST + PRACTICAL + SIGN-OFF
Every level is earned the same way — an online test for the knowledge, a practical assessment on the floor, then a sign-off. You only move up when both are done, and only the right person can sign.
1 · TRAINEE   IN TRAINING
Online — safety & values test
Practical — fit helmets, do belts, greet & read the room (supervised)
Sign-off — Track Manager
2 · ROOKIE   RUNS RACES
Online — full Part One test
Practical — run a session solo — briefing, signals, recovery, de-brief
Sign-off — Track Manager
3 · RALLY   TRACK LEAD
Online — leadership + Part One test
Practical — Speed 4 qualified (2 races, 0 spins, 6 laps under the Speed 4 time) + lead a shift
Sign-off — Track Manager
4 · WRC   TRACK MANAGER
Online — Part One + Part Two test
Practical — incidents, Education Score & developing Leads
Sign-off — Senior Track Manager (State) and/or Daymon & GMac
5 · DRIVER COACH  SENIOR LEADERS
Online — standards & leadership review
Practical — proven record across venues; develops Track Managers
Sign-off — Daymon & GMac only
★ · THE GMAC STANDARD   DAYMON & GMAC
The gold standard — recognised, not applied for. Awarded by Daymon & GMac.
No sign-off, no level up.  ·  8 / 51
practicalCriteria: Explains the model: online test + practical + sign-off
States the pass mark: 100% on safety, 90% overall

modules.title: The Briefing
content: EVERY WORD IS THE BRAND.
READ THE ROOM.
THE BRIEFING
VERBAL FIRST, THEN THE VIDEO — SET IT UP FOR SUCCESS
Deliver the briefing in the Driver Zone — yellow stools, watch the video.

You can deliver a verbal briefing before or after the video starts. How well you engage people now has a huge impact on how well the race runs. Every team member on shift must know how to deliver a briefing — and understand what they're saying.
The Briefing Zone is the yellow area of the Driver Zone — guests sit on the yellow stools and watch the video here.
Be genuinely positive. Lead with "we're here to help you have a great time and go quick." Read the room — name the birthdays; first-timers get "you're going to love this."
The briefing is where the experience is set up for success. Phones down, make sure everyone's paying attention, and politely ask anyone who’s attention is drifting to re-focus on the video.
Miss it now, fix it all session.  ·  9 / 51
BLUNT · FUNNY WITHOUT TRYING · NEVER PRECIOUS
PRE-RACE
BRIEFING
We're here to help them
go as fast as they want to go.
This guide is your script for the verbal briefing. Understand it. Be confident and deliver it every time. The pre-race briefing sets up the whole session.
YOUR TWO PRIORITIES
1   KEEP PEOPLE SAFE
2   DELIVER THE EXPERIENCE
THE PRE-RACE BRIEFING SETS UP THE WHOLE RACE.
Miss it now, fix it all session.
Every team member must know how to deliver the briefing — and understand what they're saying.
The briefing sets up the experience.
VERBAL FIRST → THEN THE VIDEO
DELIVERY TIPS
SET THE TONE FIRST
BEFORE YOU START
DELIVERY TIPS — SET THE TONE
These tips are not optional – 100% delivered 100% of the time. How you deliver the briefing matters as much as what you say.
MAKE EYE CONTACT & SMILE
Set a fun, welcoming tone before you say a word. The more open you are the more open the people you are talking to are likely to be.
SPEAK CLEARLY

Relaxed pace. Don't rush. Understand exactly what you are saying - if you don’t understand you need to ask questions until  you do. Remember everyone you are delivering to is hearing the briefing for the first time.
READ THE ROOM

Ask if they've been to a PowerPlay before or been go-karting before. Adjust your energy to match them. Some people may have their own helmet, or some people may look nervous. Their body language tells you something. Pick up what that means for how you tackle the session.
USE THE ARROWS ON TRACK
Point to the arrows on the wall/floor as you explain each one.
THE BRIEFING SCRIPT
GREETING & POWER
THE BRIEFING SCRIPT
DELIVER NATURALLY — COVER EVERY POINT
GREETING
SAY →
“Hey guys — first time here for everyone?” (open hands, wait for the nod)
“Any of you been go-karting before?” If yes, ask where — gauge experience.
“Anyone got their own helmet or balaclava? Looks like we’ve got some pros in the room!”
SAY →
“Welcome to PowerPlay! I’ve got a short safety video in a sec.”
“First, a few quick tips — to keep you safe and help you go as fast as you want.”
“I’m sure some of you want to top the leaderboard?” (point to it) “We are here to help you get you there!”
INSTANT POWER
SAY →
“These karts are the best we could find from europe and have instant power — press the green pedal or the accelerator and it’s there straight away.”
“If you push hard enough you’ll spin the wheels, drift and slide, all great fun”
“So you’ll want to control the way you accelerate — I’ll show you how shortly.”
Miss it now, fix it all session.
THE BRIEFING SCRIPT
THE ARROWS — KEY SAFETY POINT
THE ARROWS
KEY SAFETY POINT — COVER THIS EVERY TIME
SAY →
“The secret to going fast? The brakes — the red pedal. Used right, they help you turn – don’t believe me, ill explain how shortly.”
“You’ll see coloured arrows at every corner” (point to them).
“They show where to drive — and each colour is designed to give you clues on what to do:”
COLOUR
MEANING
WHAT TO TELL CUSTOMERS
RED
BRAKING ZONE

The Red Arrow is the braking zone. Turn the steering toward the corner, then brake — enough to help the kart change direction. Need it to turn more? A touch harder and a little longer on the brake pedal will do that. Hear a screech? Ease off the brake a little.
YELLOW
CONTROL POWER

At the Yellow Arrows you can start to squeeze on the accelerator – use your hand to show what that means. Manage your speed through the corner. If the kart starts to slide or spin, you’ve pressed the accelerator a bit too hard — lift off and it'll straighten up. Spin in this part of the corner, it just means you're a touch heavy on the throttle – otherwise know as a lead foot .
BLACK
FLAT OUT!

The Black Arrow is where you should be able to go flat out on the accelerator. All you have to make sure of is that you have the kart pointed in the direction you want to go. – Use your hands to show what you mean – then go as hard as you want on the accelerator / green pedal.
RED = BRAKE  ·  YELLOW = CONTROL THE POWER  ·  BLACK = FLAT OUT – USE YOUR HANDS IN THE BRIEFING
THE BRIEFING SCRIPT
RAMP & CLOSE
THE RAMP & CLOSE
FINISH STRONG — EVERY TIME
THE RAMP — LAST TIP
SAY →
“Last tip — brake as you come down the ramp. Trust me, you don’t want to meet the wall.”
{Write a specific line for your track here — every ramp is different.}
CLOSE & HAND TO VIDEO
SAY →
“Out on track you’ll see us talking and using hand signals — our team does that to try and help you while you’re driving. Keep an eye out for us.”
“Thanks for listening! I’ll put the video on now — it shows more of the techniques we just discussed.”
“When it’s done, I’ll come help you with your helmets.”
COMMON CUSTOMER QUESTIONS
Can I drift?
Yes! The karts have plenty of power to drift. If you find yourself drifting or sliding too much, lift off the power and the kart will come back straight.
What if I spin out?
We’ll come and help you if you are stuck. Sometimes you will be able to move on your own even when you think you are stuck – turn the steering and try to move.
How do I go faster?
Follow the arrows, brake early and smooth, control that accelerator out of the corner. Braking earlier gives you more chance to get the corner right.
Can I bump other karts?
Rubbing is racing — but be respectful. No intentional ramming of others.
QUICK REFERENCE
RUN THROUGH THIS BEFORE EVERY BRIEFING
QUICK REFERENCE
RUN THROUGH THIS BEFORE EVERY BRIEFING
Welcome — first time? raced before? own helmet? (spot the pros)
Instant power — control the throttle; too sideways, lift off
Braking into corners — turn toward the corner and brake to help it turn. Horrible noise – braked too hard.
Red arrow = Braking zone
Yellow arrow = control power — start to squeeze the throttle
Black arrow = flat out — as fast as you want to go
Warn about the ramp — brake before the last arrow (softer & earlier = better corner)
Mention hand signals & voice on track — then play the video, return, helmets
practicalCriteria: Sets up in the Briefing Zone (yellow stools, video); gets attention, phones down
Delivers the verbal briefing using the script: greeting & power, the arrows, ramp & close
Explains the arrows correctly — red = brake, yellow = control the power, black = flat out
Re-focuses drifting guests politely; stays genuinely positive and reads the room
Runs the quick-reference check before the briefing

modules.title: Helmets and Safety
content: 100% SAFETY,
100% OF THE TIME.
HELMETS & SAFETY
IT STARTS WITH THE RIGHT HELMET
SAFETY IS OUR TOP PRIORITY — 100% SAFETY, 100% OF THE TIME. IT STARTS WITH CHOOSING THE CORRECT HELMET.
BEFORE FITTING
Most guests have never worn a helmet — help them choose the right size.
Remove glasses before fitting — back on once the helmet's on.
Some head scarves fit under a balaclava. Turbans generally  cannot.
SIZING & FIT
You help them pick their helmet size. Left alone people choose by colour or go too big in size.
Must be tight around the chin and face.
Too loose? Size down until it fits snug.
D-BUCKLE & CHECK
Our helmets use a D-buckle fastening system — far safer than a clip.
Done up under the chin nice and tight, it must not come off over the chin.
Confirm with the two-finger test.
IF THEY REFUSE
Show them how it can come off the chin if loose.
Still won't wear it right? They cannot race.
Inform your Team Lead — they process the refund.
Stay respectful — they’re disappointed, not difficult.
RE-CHECK AT PIT LANE

Even if you fitted the helmet, check it again at the entry to pit lane — 100% of the time. Engage with the customer, ask their kart number, re-check the helmet, and guide them to the right side. Customers should always walk on the yellow side.
100% safety, 100% of the time.  ·  16 / 51
practicalCriteria: Selects the correct helmet size
Fastens the D-buckle correctly
Performs the two-finger test
Handles an ill-fitting helmet or refusal appropriately

modules.title: Setting Up In The Kart
content: BELTS FIRST. THEN SEAT.
THEN PEDALS.
SETTING UP IN THE KART
ENTER FROM THE YELLOW SIDE · BELTS, SEAT, PEDALS
GETTING IN & STRAPPED
Enter from the yellow painted side of pit lane. Fine to stand on the seat — never on the plastic.
Karts are reset between races if you have time: seat back, pedals forward, belts extended.
Thread both arms through the belts. Waist strap done up first, then the two shoulder straps. Every belt tight.
Always check a customer's belts even if they look right, double check them. Talk to them while you do it.
WAIST STRAP
ACROSS THE WAIST

This matters more than anything. The waist strap must sit low and across the waist, never across the stomach or chest. Always double check seat belts even as you send someone from pit lane you can do a quick visual check.
SEAT, PEDALS & CONTROL

The correct seating position has an upward bend in the legs and a downward bend in the arms. This allows people to squeeze the pedals rather than treating them like a light switch. Straight legs and arms equals less control. Being able to vary the pressure on the pedals whilst racing is the secret to a fast lap time – this is the technique.

As a general guide you will find the correct position is arms stretched straight ahead while belted in, a person’s wrist should be over the top of the steering wheel. Then seat (red strap between legs) and pedals (red button under wheel). Note some body types, long legs or torso will mean this doesn’t always work.

Always talk to people while you are strapping them in. It’s your chance to build rapport. Once you have belted someone in, turn the kart on by twisting the red button. Lights on = all safety checked & race-ready.
No belt, no track.  ·  17 / 51
practicalCriteria: Does belts up with the waist strap low and checks tightness
Sets belts first, then seat, then pedals
Confirms the driver is secure before sending
Walks on the yellow; stays safe around moving karts

modules.title: Trainee Certification
content: KNOW IT.
PROVE IT.
LEVEL 1 · TRAINEE
IN TRAINING — CERTIFICATION
TYPICAL DURATION:  3 × 3-hour sessions
ONLINE TEST
The two priorities & the PRACE values
The customer is #1: Respect, Help, Interact
Helmets, belts & the two-finger test
Kart operation: charging, pit gate, on/off, sending karts
The safety briefing
Getting in safely; always walk on the yellow
Pass:  100% on safety questions, 90% overall.
PRACTICAL ASSESSMENT
Delivers the safety briefing
Driving: laps within the Speed 4 time + 25%
Plugs karts in and out (charging)
Sends karts onto the track; works the pit gate
Turns karts on and off
Fits helmets & does belts (waist strap low); two-finger test
Greets guests, reads the room, walks them on the yellow
Never tells a customer off
SIGNED OFF BY:  Track Manager
Trainee → Rookie  ·  46 / 51

syllabus: Everything in Trainee
The verbal briefing + matching the speech to the group
All seven hand signals and their meaning
Less is more: react to cues, speak only when needed
The fast line + reading the kart & driver
Running the race + the three-chance principle
Bringing them in, the de-brief, Speeds 1–4 & the controller
Pass:  100% on safety questions, 90% overall.

modules.title: Pre-Race Speeches
content: BLUNT · FUNNY WITHOUT TRYING · NEVER PRECIOUS
PRE-RACE SPEECH IN PIT LANE
Know what to say.
Before every race.

Use the right speech for the right group. The pre-race speech sets up how well the race runs, it’s a final word to help people before they head out on track. Not a lecture — it’s all about helping our customers.

ONE SPEECH PRIOR TO THE FIRST RACE  ·  THEN A DIFFERENT SPEECH PRIOR TO THEIR SECOND AND FOLLOWING RACES. MAYBE YOU HAVE A RACE WITH A MIXTURE OF PEOPLE DOING THEIR FIRST OR SECOND RACE IN THAT CASE COMBINE THE BEST OF BOTH SPEECHES
READ THE ROOM.
PICK THE SPEECH.
There are two first race speech options
And five following race options — each one targets a specific driving issue you spotted in the previous race.
SMOOTHER RACES, BETTER LAP TIMES & BIGGER SMILES ARE THE REWARD.
PRE-RACE SPEECH
FIRST RACE
FIRST RACE SPEECHES
CHOOSE THE RIGHT ONE FOR YOUR GROUP
Option 1  —  For most groups
SAY →
“Heads up — your first couple of laps on track will be slippery while the tyres warm up.”
“Be smooth on the accelerator out of corners and remember to brake on the red arrows.”
“Get the technique right and the tyres will warm up and give you more grip — so you go faster. The key is braking earlier than you think!”
“No one wants to be first person to spin so use the first couple of laps to get use to the track. Everyone ready to go?”
Option 2  —  For majority juniors or nervous / inexperienced drivers
SAY →
“Let’s do a quick practice first. Turn the wheel hard right… and hard left…great job….one more time, always keep turning never give up”
“Now lets squeeze the green pedal down — that’s the go pedal. Always squeeze it, never stomp on it, the smoother the better.”
“Hard press on the red pedal — that’s the brake. Use it on the red arrows, down the ramp, anywhere you want to slow down.”
“Any questions? Everyone ready to go?”
READ THE ROOM — PICK THE OPTION THAT FITS YOUR GROUP. IF ONE PERSON LOOKS NERVOUS THEN PLEASE HELP THEM INDIVIDUALLY.
PRE-RACE SPEECH
FOLLOWING RACES
THE SECOND (or more) RACE SPEECHES
SPOT THE ISSUE IN THE FIRST RACE AND USE THESE BETWEEN RACES — USE THE RIGHT SPEECH
Option 1  —  Guests aren't sliding into corners / barely using the brakes
SAY →
“Great race everyone! On quick tip, the brake does most of the turning, not the steering wheel.”
“Turn the wheel toward the corner and brake on the red arrows. Not turning enough? Brake a bit harder.”
“Master it and you get back on the green pedal / accelerator earlier out of the corner — that’s the key to faster lap times. Ready?”
Option 2  —  Guests sliding too much or stopping completely when braking
SAY →
“Good work last race! One tip to go faster:”
“If you are almost stopping in the middle of the corner it means you’ve braked a bit too hard.”
“To stop that as you enter the corner, you want to ease off the brake — then you won’t stop, and you can get back on the power earlier. Ready?”
Option 3  —  Guests hugging the inside of corners
SAY →
“Great race! Want to go faster in the next race? Remember to drive over the top of the arrows through the corners.”
“Miss the arrows and you make the track longer —  the more distance you travel the slower the lap times.”
“If you stay on the arrows, especially into the corner — it sets up a better exit making it easier to steer. Ready?”
PRE-RACE SPEECH
FOLLOWING RACES
THE SECOND (or more) RACE SPEECHES
SPOT THE ISSUE — USE THE RIGHT SPEECH
Option 4  —  Guests not controlling power in the corner / over-sliding out of corners
SAY →
“Great race! Quick tip, if you find your sliding too much on the way out of corners?”
“Then try and be smooth on the accelerator — squeeze it, don’t treat it like a light switch.”
“Sliding on the exit loses speed down the straight. Smooth = more grip = faster exit, higher top speed and better lap times. Ready?”
Option 5  —  Guests turning too late or hitting the back of other drivers
SAY →
“Good race! Quick tip, one thing to try and focus on is to look further ahead through the corners and down the straight.”
“The further ahead you look, the more time you have to react and prepare — and it smooths out your steering.”
“You’ll also spot trouble earlier, like your friend (wife, work mate, bf etc) spun out  . Smoother steering, means you prepare earlier, you’ll fo faster and get better lap times. Ready?”
READ THE ROOM — PICK THE OPTION THAT FITS YOUR GROUP. OR MAYBE SPEAK TO INDIVIDUALS WHILE THE KARTS ARE CHARGING
AS IT MAY JUST BE ONE PERSON THAT IS STRUGGLING. THE RIGHT TIP MEANS BIGGER SMILES AND SMOOTHER RACES
practicalCriteria: Delivers the pit-lane speech matched to the group (first race / following races)
Keeps it a helpful final word — not a lecture
Adjusts tone for adults, kids and corporate groups

modules.title: Talk With Your Hands — Hand Signals
content: HANDS + VOICE.
NEVER YELL.
TALK WITH YOUR HANDS
ON TRACK — LOUD VOICE OVER THE MOTOR + CLEAR SIGNALS

Use hand signals on track, alongside your voice — they make it far easier for people to understand you. Speak loudly so they can hear you over the noise of the motor and the tyres, and back every call with a clear hand signal. Never yell at people; point, show and signal.
POINT AT THEM
Get their attention if they should be braking and aren't.
ONE HAND UP
Means brake — now. Clear, calm, unmistakable.
PULL HAND BACK
Ease off — brake if sliding into the corner, throttle if sliding out of the corner.
POINT THE LINE
Show them where to drive — wide on the entry. On the arrows if marked.
LOOK UP
Vision further up the road. Raise an arm, palm up — looking further ahead smooths their steering.
TURN IN HARD
Two hands gripping the wheel, turning harder. Get the front pointed where you want to go.
THUMBS UP + CLAP
Yes! Cheer them on the moment they nail it.
If you're shouting "brake" every corner, you're not coaching — you're a robot with a microphone. GMac will not be happy.
Signal early. Stay calm.  ·  22 / 51
practicalCriteria: Uses all seven hand signals correctly
Signals clearly so drivers can read them from across the track
Uses hands + voice; never yells

modules.title: Less Is More
content: OBSERVE FIRST.
SPEAK ONLY IF NEEDED.
LESS IS MORE
ON TRACK — READ THE GROUP, SPEAK WITH PURPOSE WHEN NEEDED

Less is more when you are on track. You’re there to observe, keep people safe and speak as you need to so that you help people — not to shout “brake” every lap. Read the group, then decide what to say. Remember to use your hands.
READ THE PLAY
Every group is different. Watch first, then decide what each group needs. We call “brake” only if we don’t see them braking — not for the sake of it every lap.
For example, if they don’t brake enough use hand signals and say brake harder together. Same for steering or too much throttle. Use your hands and limited words.
PEOPLE, NOT PROCESS

It’s a people-person job — you need to be at least somewhat outgoing. Be people-oriented, not process-oriented. It’s all about their experience: keep them safe first, then help them on track, then engage with them off track.
Never default to going back and resetting karts. Engage with customers – the De-brief is everything. Re-setting karts is only a nice to have if you have time.
HANDS FIRST
Pointing and hand signals are the best way to get people’s attention on track.

With a helmet on it’s super hard to hear — you can’t hold a conversation while they race. Hand Signals and a few words – Brake Harder, Brake, Brake Earlier, Brake Softer, Ease off the Brake,  Turn More, Turn Harder, Turn Earlier, Keep Steering, Look Up,
REACT TO VISUAL CUES

React to what you see, not a script. Bottom of the ramp, first lap: watch they’re braking — you only need to shout brake if they haven’t braked when you think should have or if you have a nervous customer that needs encouragement, kids party that you are concerned about or other examples like that.
Otherwise Trust people, observe, and speak up if they don’t do the right thing.
READ THE PLAY: lap one you send out a nervous group — how do you tell they’re nervous, and what changes? You send out a kids party — what are you thinking next?
Less is more — read the play.  ·  23 / 51
practicalCriteria: Reacts to cues without over-coaching

modules.title: Roles On Track
content: TWO ON TRACK.
READ IT FROM BOTH SIDES.
ROLES ON TRACK
WHO STANDS WHERE DURING A RACE
In peak periods there are two people on track. Both coach with signals, both cover recoveries in their zone.
POSITION 1 · BOTTOM OF THE RAMP
THE CRITICAL BRAKING POINT
Watch their feet — first three laps especially.
Speak loudly only if you don’t see them braking.
Or if you have a nervous group then speak loudly.
Want them braking earlier? Point at them and say it, clear and loud
Leads the race – sets the tone in the first few laps to set the race up.
Focus – keeping people safe first laps, correct braking and/or steering then move as more confident into helping and educating.
POSITION 2 · PRE-DETERMINED LOCATION
SET BY THE TRACK MANAGER FOR YOUR LAYOUT – APPROVED BY GMAC
The next place that influences the success of how races are run.
Read the race from the other side — signals first, a few words only.
Proactive, loud and clear for juniors or anyone nervous.
Recoveries in your zone — stand on the safe side.
Focus – safety and teaching people the technique – get them to slide. Keep the race running, limited stoppages yet helping and education are the key to bigger smiles.
AROUND THE RACE
Front of House — one FOH person can jump on track to help  if the race is struggling (e.g. a kids or junior race).
Recoveries in your zone — always assess the track before entering it. Stand on the safe side away from traffic when you can.
If they spin in the first half of the corner they braked too hard.
If the spin the second half of the corner they accelerated too hard.
PEAK TIMES = TWO ON TRACK.  Quieter periods = one — positioned at the bottom of the ramp.
Stand where the race is won.  ·  24 / 51
practicalCriteria: Knows the two on-track roles and who stands where
Moves to the right position during the race

modules.title: The Fast Line
content: IT'S NOT HOW FAST YOU GO.
IT'S HOW MUCH FASTER THAN YOUR MATES.
THE FAST LINE
WHAT TO ACTUALLY COACH
Brake earlier than they think — especially down the ramp - if your track has a hairpin at the bottom). This gives more time to get the corner right.
"Brake hard" is the wrong cue. Braking takes a decent amount of force — you should brake hard enough to help change direction — but how much depends on the driver (height & weight) speed and corner.

Brake too late and they'll brake really hard -  the horrible screech will tell you when this happens - to make the corner. Brake earlier and the same stop stays smooth and controlled. Faster and quieter. If you don’t think so – you haven’t mastered the technique – keep practicing.
Wide on entry — then steer in as they brake so the kart slides a little into the corner. Only happens if they are going fast enough.
Braking & steering shift weight so the kart changes direction quickly — on our track it should slide a little. (because it is slippery)
Slide in a little into the corner, drive straight out. Being earlier on the throttle and having a straight exit is where the speed and lap time comes from. It is all about technique not braking late.
The front of the kart is an arrow — get it turned and pointing where you want to go, then when it is facing the way you want to go you can get back on the green pedal.

Look far ahead — not at their feet or just ahead, but much further up the track. You will prepare and turn earlier. As well as seeing your mates that have spun out earlier. Jerky steering means they are looking too close.
WIDE IN · CLIP THE APEX · STRAIGHT OUT
The surface decides who's telling the truth.  ·  25 / 51
practicalCriteria: Coaches the fast line accurately
Frames it as “faster than your mates,” not just fast

modules.title: Reading The Kart and Driver
content: SEE IT. NAME THE CAUSE.
GIVE THE FIX.
READING THE KART & DRIVER ON TRACK
SEE IT · REALISE THE CAUSE · HELP CORRECT IT — WITHIN A FEW LAPS
What you see / hear | What it means | The fix to signal
Slides a lot before the corner or spins before the corner | Braked too hard | Ease off the brake — the kart straightens and won't spin or go as sideways.
Spins from mid-corner to the exit | Too much throttle | Ease off the throttle — same again, it straightens and won't spin.
Runs wide / overshoots the corner – misses the apex | Braked too late | Brake a touch earlier next lap — more time to get it right.
Horrible screech | Locked the brake / too hard | Brake earlier and a bit softer. Not later.
Can't get the kart to slide at all | Not yet fast enough | Encourage more speed — or just help them round if they are nervous and don't want to go faster.
Pressing both pedals (double-pedalling) | Brake + throttle together | Fine for a confident driver; if it compromises them, one pedal at a time.
Jerky / rough steering. Reacting very late to corners — karts are unstable. | Looking too close — just in front of the kart | Looking further ahead will smooth out all steering.
Spins whilst turning into the corner | Turned too sharp into a corner | Looking further ahead will smooth out the steering.
↳ Keep reinforcing it — don't just say it once. A too-grippy track lets people brake late and get away with it — that's grip, not speed.
Fewer stoppages = a better experience.  ·  26 / 51
practicalCriteria: Spots the problem (see it)
Names the cause
Gives the correct fix

modules.title: Running The Race
content: WATCH THE TRACK.
NOT THE SCOREBOARD.
RUNNING THE RACE
BOTH ATTENDANTS VOCAL — MANAGE IT, DON'T WATCH THE TIMER – THE FIRST THREE LAPS ARE THE KEY
YOUR JOB DURING THE RACE
The first three laps are critical to get control of the race. Set the tone. Manage the experience. If you have multiple spins, ask yourself why and what you can do to fix it.
Is to not stare at the scoreboard or the tablet worrying when the race ends. You should be watching the track, keeping people safe and educate.
If there are two attendants on track both should be vocal. A silent attendant isn't helping deliver the experience.
No matter who's racing, you can help. Know how many groups are in the race, it changes how you manage it.
ENCOURAGE — NEVER CRITICISE
Don't tell people off. There's no sign at PowerPlay that says "DON'T" — so no negative language. Positive.
Front-of-house: step in when a race isn't running smoothly. Be part of the solution — never criticise a team member.
Reflect and act. Talk with your team member between races about how you can improve the races and help people more.
Educate yourself on what to look for so that you can spot how to help people during a race.
MORE THAN 4–5 STOPPAGES A RACE?

You need to change how you're managing the race. The number of stoppages tells you whether you're doing a good job. Stoppages are frustrating and affect the experience. It means people don’t have as good a time and this affects the word of mouth advertising.

Word of mouth is one of the biggest drivers of PowerPlay's success — guests pick how they heard about us when they register (order randomised), we measure it, and it is a reflection on how well the experience is delivered.
The stoppage count tells you how you're doing.  ·  27 / 51
practicalCriteria: Watches the track, not the scoreboard
Manages the first three laps
Keeps the stoppage count down

modules.title: Recovery and Stoppages
content: KEEP IT FLOWING.
STOP-START KILLS IT.
RECOVERY & STOPPAGES
GET THEM GOING — KEEP THE RACE FLOWING – STOPPAGES STOP THE EXPERIENCE HAPPENING

Stop-start ruins the experience. Some stoppages are inevitable — but repeated stoppages are unacceptable; it means you've lost control of the race. Educate more. We get the race running again quickly.
KARTS TO SPEED 1
Put all karts on Speed 1 if needed — normally required. 100% safety, 100% of the time. If all karts just passed the spun kart/s use common sense. Stay Alert.
STAND SAFE
Stand on the side of the kart away from approaching traffic. Never stand between a spun kart and oncoming drivers.
LET CUSTOMERS SELF-RESCUE
If they can recover by turning more and brushing a barrier, encourage it — back racing faster. You don’t need to Speed 1 in this case.
QUICK WHY, THEN GO
Help them grasp what happened as you recover them, fast and clearly, before sending them back to racing.
THE THREE-CHANCE PRINCIPLE — APPLIES TO INDIVIDUALS
1   2   3   →   OFF THE TRACK TO COACH

If one person spins or causes stoppages three times, bring them off to educate before they rejoin — this race, the next, or a solo session. Unlike other experiences at PowerPlay someone spinning or causing stoppages can make it less fun for others. People get frustrated by stoppages which is completely fair enough. Give people 3 chances. If they are still causing a race to be slowed down, take them off track, re-educate them in the pit lane. Then send them back out in the race if you are confident they understood or send them out in between the next race or have them join a different race if time allows. We cannot have one person compromising another person’s experience.
Everyone gets the experience they paid for.  ·  28 / 51
practicalCriteria: Uses Speed 1 for recovery
Stands on the safe side
Applies the three-chance principle
Avoids stop-start; keeps the race flowing

modules.title: Bringing Them In
content: SLOW SPEED ZONE.
STILL DANGEROUS.
BRINGING THEM IN
END OF RACE — MANAGE PIT LANE CAREFULLY
PUSH WHO NEEDS IT
Push the juniors in, and any drivers you're concerned about. Talk to them as you push them in. One hand on the roll bar and one on the steering wheel. Never just behind the kart.
EVERYONE ELSE DRIVES IN
Adults, and Speed 3 or 4 approved juniors, can drive into pit lane themselves. Guide them to which side you want them to park on. They should park off the painted surfaces.
SWITCH OFF ON STOP
Switch off each kart as it comes to a stop. Only use the Emergency Stop button on the controller in an emergency
STAY SEATED
Ask everyone to remain seated until all karts are in pit lane. You do not want a kart hitting another kart and knocking someone over as they get out of the go-kart.
PLUG IN TO CHARGE
Plug the karts in as soon as you can so they charge between races. This should happen before re-setting belts.
THEN THEY EXIT
Once all karts are in pit lane, inform people they can get out of the karts. Help them with the buckle if needed. And guide them onto the yellow side and exit back into the driver zone.
ALWAYS COMMUNICATING – LOUD – PEOPLE HAVE HELMETS ON
Pit lane is a slow-speed zone — but it's dangerous, and must be managed carefully.
Manage pit lane carefully — it's still dangerous.  ·  29 / 51
practicalCriteria: Treats the slow-speed zone as still dangerous
Brings drivers in and manages pit lane safely

modules.title: Kart Speeds and The Controller
content: ONE CONTROLLER.
FULL CONTROL OF THE RACE.
KART SPEEDS & THE HAND HELD CONTROLLER
THE CONTROLLER RUNS THE RACE — KNOW EVERY MODE

The hand-held controller is how you run a safe, smooth race. Know the speed modes, how to slow or stop karts, and when to use each one. Placeholder detail below — confirm your real speeds and controls and I'll finalise.
SPEED 1
Slowest — recovery & getting karts moving safely. 100% safety.
SPEED 2
Juniors / nervous drivers — controlled pace.
SPEED 3
Standard racing speed for most sessions.
SPEED 4
Top speed — qualified drivers, leads & managers.
USING THE CONTROLLER
◆  Slow the whole field to Speed 1 when you need to recover a kart or regain control.
◆  Individual karts can be slowed or stopped — use this rather than stopping the whole race where you can.
◆  Emergency Stop only in a genuine emergency. Confirm your venue's exact buttons and modes and I'll lock this in.
Know every mode before you run a race.
practicalCriteria: Sets kart speeds 1–4 correctly
Uses the controller confidently for full control of the race

modules.title: The De-Brief
content: YOU ARE THE PRODUCT.
NOT THE KART. NOT THE SURFACE.
THE DE-BRIEF – DRIVER ZONE
THE DE-BRIEF AREA — THE MOST IMPORTANT PART
BEFORE THEY GET OUT OF THE KART
"How'd you go? Did you have fun? What time did you do?" Start the chat while it's hot.
TO THE DE-BRIEF
ZONE
Help them take helmets off in the Driver Zone. Never make them line up outside the Driver Zone. The De Brief is all about connection.
PLACE THE
RESULT
Help them see where they finished. Compliment what they nailed. Give them tips on how they can improve.
GAUGE THE VIBE
This is where you read whether they had a great time and act on it. Chat with them. Understand why they did or didn’t have a great time.
IF THEY'RE NOT BUZZING
Underwhelmed or unhappy? Engage with them — don't let it slide past. GMac wants everyone to leave happy.
Ask yourself: was the race stop / start? Too many stoppages kills the experience.
Offer credit for a bad race. The Team Lead on shift authorises it — use the term 'Disrupted Race DD/MM/YY'.
Still need help? Ask your track or team lead to intervene.
THE CONCEPT OF POWERPLAY
THEY ARRIVE WITH A SMILE — THEY LEAVE WITH A BIGGER ONE.
If we skip the de-brief the memory doesn't form — they just drove karts – that is not PowerPlay.
The feeling people have in the De-Brief is what we sell. If the de-brief doesn’t happen we didn’t deliver the experience. Then the brand didn’t happen.
The Debrief area is about creating connection and 'bonded moments'. It is real — PowerPlay, when the experience is delivered correctly, turns people into little kids. That’s why people come back.
The scoreboard will let us know how good you are.  ·  30 / 51
practicalCriteria: Gives the result, genuine praise, and one tip
Owns the delivery — “you are the product”

modules.title: The De-Brief Between Races
content: THE RACE ENDS.
THE EXPERIENCE DOESN'T.
THE DE-BRIEF BETWEEN RACES
KEEP THEM BUZZING BETWEEN HEATS — DON'T LOSE THE CONNECTION
Between races is still the experience. Multi-race bookings live or die on what you do between heats — keep the energy up, give them something to improve, and make them want the next race.
KEEP THEM ENGAGED
◆  Don't let them drift to their phones. Keep talking — energy stays high between heats.
◆  Quick, specific praise: name the corner they nailed or the time they beat.
GIVE THEM A GOAL
◆  One thing to work on next race — brake a touch earlier, look further ahead.
◆  Tell them their tyres will have more grip next race, so they can go quicker.
SET UP THE NEXT RACE
◆  Remind them of the running order / who's ahead — build the rivalry.
◆  Get them seated and ready in good time so the next heat starts cleanly.
This page is a starting point — tell me how you run between-race de-briefs at PowerPlay and I'll build it out with your exact wording.
Keep them buzzing between heats.
practicalCriteria: Resets and lifts drivers for the next race
Keeps the experience alive between races

modules.title: Rookie Certification
content: KNOW IT.
PROVE IT.
LEVEL 2 · ROOKIE
RUNS RACES — CERTIFICATION
TYPICAL DURATION:  3 × 3-hour sessions
ONLINE TEST
Everything in Trainee
The verbal briefing + matching the speech to the group
All seven hand signals and their meaning
Less is more: react to cues, speak only when needed
The fast line + reading the kart & driver
Running the race + the three-chance principle
Bringing them in, the de-brief, Speeds 1–4 & the controller
Pass:  100% on safety questions, 90% overall.
PRACTICAL ASSESSMENT
Delivers a clear verbal briefing and gets attention
Matches the pre-race speech to the group
Straps everyone in (waist strap low); re-checks at pit lane
Uses signals + a loud voice; calls brake only if needed
Watches their feet down the ramp; reacts to cues
Manages a recovery safely from the safe side
Keeps the race under the stoppage limit
Runs a genuine de-brief: result, praise, one tip
SIGNED OFF BY:  Track Manager
Rookie → Rally  ·  47 / 51

syllabus: Everything in Rookie
Leading the floor & coaching the coaches
Front of house helping the track
Incidents & faults: report it
The Education Score: what it is and how it moves
Equipment: track, karts, batteries, barriers
Pass:  100% on safety questions, 90% overall.

modules.title: Front Of House — Helping The Track
content: ONE TEAM.
ONE EXPERIENCE.
FRONT OF HOUSE: HELPING THE TRACK
ONE TEAM — FRONT OF HOUSE AND TRACK DELIVER THE EXPERIENCE TOGETHER
Front of House and the Track Team are one team. When you're not with a customer, your eyes and hands help the track deliver the experience.
SET UP THE RACE
◆  Put notes in every race: which groups are with who, who's getting out, who's staying in.
◆  Take people over at the right time — with 5 minutes left on the race clock everyone should be seated and the verbal briefing starting.
◆  Start the heat if the Track Team have forgotten to.
WATCH & COMMUNICATE
◆  Note anyone struggling on track; radio the Track Team if you think they've missed it.
◆  One FOH person can help on track if a race is struggling — e.g. a Kids Birthday or Junior race.
◆  Call out any kart stopped on the ramp straight away.
◆  Listen for noise — a bang or a barrier hit, note who and why. Same person hitting barriers? Tell the team they're struggling.
INCIDENTS & THE DE-BRIEF
◆  Watch for incidents and respond quickly — for a major incident, meet the guest over at the Driver Zone.
◆  Help take helmets off and complete the de-brief if needed.
◆  Help fit helmets when the Track Team is busy.
◆  Talk to customers about their experience, show them the results screens, and encourage podium photos.
◆  Keep the on-track team hydrated, and push karts into pit lane for Kids/Junior races if you have time.
When you're free, help the track.
practicalCriteria: Works front of house and track as one team
Steps between roles to keep the experience flowing

modules.title: The Equipment
content: THE BEST KIT IN THE WORLD.
THAT'S NOT A SLOGAN.
THE EQUIPMENT
THE INVESTMENT & CONCEPT — THE BEST WE COULD FIND
THE TRACK
From Italy — far exceeds Australian safety standards. Safety is No. 1.
~200m long.
Designed for a 20-second lap so you get more chances to improve.
Surface is purposely slippery.
THE KARTS
From France, fully electric.
Seat and pedals both move. Highly adjustable.
Karts 1 & 2 have a larger seat.
~220kg each kart before the driver gets in.
THE INVESTMENT & CONCEPT
$750K+ — track + karts together.
The best of everything, sourced worldwide. Safety is No. 1.
Huge scoreboard — it's fun, but it's also competitive. Everyone wants to beat someone.

Why is the track slippery? GMac wanted PowerPlay to feel a bit like driving a world rally car on snow or gravel — a taste of what he felt across nearly 30 years in the co-driver's seat, competing all over the world. Things that are a challenge are way more fun — being slippery means technique is rewarded, and it lets you educate people on how to do it.
The best equipment we could find anywhere.  ·  34 / 51
BUILT IN-HOUSE.
BECAUSE NOTHING ELSE CUT IT.
THE EQUIPMENT
BATTERIES, BARRIERS, CHARGING & PUSHING A KART
OUR BATTERIES
Two per kart. Lithium Iron Phosphate (LiFePO4).
Developed in-house because nothing else could provide what we wanted.
Safer than normal lithium, self-extinguishing. Could steam if it fails, unlikely, yet will never catch fire.
BARRIERS & CHARGING
PowerPlay designed rubber & metal barriers.
To increase safety we developed our own Australian-made rubber and metal barriers.
Italian chargers, three-phase power. Plugged in = won't drive.
PUSHING A KART
Keep the wheels straight when you first start to push — it makes it easier. Then turn
One hand on the roll bar, one on the steering wheel. Never just behind.
Use the opportunity whilst pushing to talk and engage with the customer.

$750K+ of the best equipment in the world. Look after it like it's yours — it's what makes the PowerPlay experience possible. Report damaged karts or barriers immediately. Safety is our number 1 priority.
Built in-house — the best we could find.  ·  35 / 51
practicalCriteria: Knows the track, karts, batteries and barriers
Handles and maintains equipment correctly

modules.title: Incidents and Equipment Faults
content: SAFETY FIRST.
ALWAYS REPORT IT.
INCIDENTS & EQUIPMENT FAULTS
REPORT IT · REVIEW IT · STOP IT HAPPENING AGAIN

Every incident and every fault gets reported. It's how we keep people safe, protect the experience, and stop the same thing happening twice. When in doubt, report it. Always review and provide solutions.
REPORT

Crashes, injuries, near-misses — and any equipment fault (kart, battery, barrier, charger). Capture what happened, when, who, the kart number, and what you did. Tell your Team Lead / Manager straight away.
REVIEW

Management reviews what happened and why — was it education, the track, the equipment, or the process? Agree what changes need to be made so it doesn't recur. Major incidents escalate to Daymon and GMac. No pointing fingers, yet we can always learn what we can do better.
RECORD & CLOSE

Log it through the venue's reporting process, take faulty karts offline until cleared, and close the loop — confirm the fix is done and the team knows. Communication is key. The Venue Management team owns the process.
HOLDING PAGE

The venue's full incident & fault reporting process — the form, who's notified, and timeframes — will be added here. Until then: report every incident and fault to your Team Lead or Manager, and don't put a questionable kart back on track.
When in doubt, report it.  ·  37 / 51
practicalCriteria: Spots incidents and faults; safety first, always reports
Keeps the experience moving while reporting

modules.title: The Education Score
content: THE NUMBER THAT
MEASURES THE EXPERIENCE.
THE EDUCATION SCORE
THE NUMBER THAT TELLS US HOW WELL WE'RE EDUCATING
The Education Score is how we measure whether we're really educating our customers. Good education means fewer spins, fewer stoppages, less kart damage — and bigger smiles. The score keeps us honest.
WHO OWNS IT & HOW IT WORKS
Ultimately owned by the Venue Manager — managed day-to-day with the Track Manager & assistants.
Monitor who needs more training before it shows up on track. Manage action plans for feedback and training.
An Education Score above 2 across a fortnight → submit a report: why, and what you're doing about it. You must provide an action plan.
Roster accordingly so more training can be conducted on shift.
HOW TO MOVE IT
Educate harder on the briefing and trackside — most of the score is won there.
Run education races in quiet times to lift the team and the track.
Check the tyre condition — when the tyres shine, there’s limited grip, which means there’s limited education happening.
Monitor the track and ensure that the grip levels are at the required standard for consistent laps. Grip tape can be applied to alter the track if required.
VENUE REPORTING

The Venue Management team will report monthly on the things that measure the experience: the education score, word of mouth, and the number of incidents. If the score is above 2 for the fortnight, then an emergency education report will be submitted with a plan on how to correct.
Good education = bigger smiles.  ·  38 / 51
practicalCriteria: Explains what the Education Score is and how it moves
Links good education to fewer spins, fewer stoppages, bigger smiles

modules.title: Rally Certification
content: KNOW IT.
PROVE IT.
LEVEL 3 · RALLY
TRACK LEAD — CERTIFICATION
TYPICAL DURATION:  min. 6 × 3-hour sessions as a Rookie
ONLINE TEST
Everything in Rookie
Leading the floor & coaching the coaches
Front of house helping the track
Incidents & faults: report it
The Education Score: what it is and how it moves
Equipment: track, karts, batteries, barriers
Pass:  100% on safety questions, 90% overall.
PRACTICAL ASSESSMENT
Speed 4 qualified: 2 races, 0 spins, 6 laps under the Speed 4 time
Leads a shift, observed by the Track Manager
Coaches a Rookie or Trainee through a session
Handles a stoppage or incident calmly and positively
Steps in when a race struggles; keeps it flowing
Models PRACE and Respect · Help · Interact
SIGNED OFF BY:  Track Manager
Rally → WRC  ·  48 / 51

levels.name: WRC
levels.signoffAuthority: Senior Track Manager (State) and/or Daymon & GMac

syllabus: Everything in Rally, plus all of Part Two
Incidents & faults: report, review, record & close
Education Score: ownership + the >2 / fortnight report
Track & karts: slippery by design; grip tape (TM/ATM/VM only)
Roles & ownership: VM, TM/ATM, Track Leads
Standards: Speed 4 monthly, race once a fortnight
Pass:  100% on safety questions, 90% overall.

modules.title: Part Two — Venue Management Team
content: PART TWO

For the Venue
Management Team.

The pages that follow are owned by the Venue Manager, Track Manager, Assistant Track Manager and Leads. Everyone is welcome to read them — but this team is accountable for managing the track, the karts, the standards and the score that protect the experience.
KEEP PEOPLE SAFE
DELIVER THE EXPERIENCE
Owned by the venue management team.  ·  36 / 51
practicalCriteria: Understands the move from delivering to owning the operation
Knows what the Venue Management Team is responsible for

modules.title: Maintaining The Track and Karts
content: THE TRACK IS EVERYTHING.
SLIPPERY BY DESIGN.
MAINTAINING THE TRACK & KARTS
TM, ATM & VM OWN THIS — IT IS THE EXPERIENCE

Without the track and the karts, there is no experience. Keeping them in the right shape is the responsibility of the Track Manager, the Assistant Track Manager and the Venue Manager. The track is meant to be slippery — that's how GMac designed it.
SLIPPERY BY DESIGN
Sliding isn't out of control — it's a skill customers love when they learn it. Watch their faces when they nail a drift.
Never try it on the open road — but the control transfers if they ever get into a real-life situation in a car.
Manage for some slide. A grippy track that drives itself isn't the experience. You will need to scrub rubber off the track or arrange for it to be ground or polished.
WHEN THE TYRES SHINE
Poor education polishes the tyres — braking late, wheel-spin, sliding all the way through.
Shiny tyres = less grip = your team's job trackside gets brutal.
Get Tyres Back by driving some races properly and / or fitting some grip tape in crucial locations.
Extra kart damage is poor education too. Fix the education first.
GRIP TAPE — TRACK MANAGER & ATM ONLY & VENUE MANAGER

Place it strategically: on the entry to a corner to help braking or through the middle of the corner where you slide, and if more is needed on the exit of the corner to catch a kart as it slides on the exit.
It wears down over time — monitor lap times and rubber build up on the edge of the track.
Advise Daymon whenever it's applied to your track.
Concerned? Escalate and create a plan to fix. Too much grip → Daymon and/or GMac, agree a plan, and you can propose a clean / grind / polish.
Slippery by design — that's GMac's intent.  ·  39 / 51
practicalCriteria: Explains why the track is “slippery by design”
Applies the grip-tape strategy (TM/ATM/VM only)
Keeps track and karts to standard

modules.title: How We Run It — Who Owns What
content: YOU ARE THE PRODUCT.
DELIVERY IS THE SECRET.
HOW WE RUN IT – WHO OWNS WHAT?
DELIVERY IS THE SECRET — WHO OWNS WHAT

The way we deliver the experience is the secret. If the team aren't engaged and helping people go as fast as they want to go — the experience never happened. Be proactive. Safety and the Experience are the priorities.
KEEP PEOPLE SAFE
DELIVER THE EXPERIENCE
VENUE MANAGER
Understands every aspect of what makes the track and the experience perfect.
Manages Front of House Team Leads becoming Speed 4 approved within a month of being appointed.
Owns the Education Score — reports on it and develops plans to improve it.
Reviews incidents on track with the Track Manager.
Reviews Word of Mouth marketing trends in venue.
TRACK MGR & ATM
Ultimately responsible for the Track and Karts. Safety first.
100% Safety 100% of the time.
Ensure the team are confident to deliver education and know what to look for.
The only ones who apply grip tape.
Assistant owner of the education score. Must be Speed 4 qualified.
Review incidents with the Venue Manager. Create Action Plans and learnings.
TRACK LEADS
Set the example. Must know how to drive to coach.
The experience lives or dies with the person leading those in front of the customer.
Must be Speed 4 approved when appointed to Track Lead. Needs to do 2 races 0 spins, 6 laps at Speed 4 time to qualify as Track Lead.
Any questions? Escalate them. Don't wait to be told.
If the team isn't engaged, it never happened.  ·  40 / 51
KEEP PEOPLE SAFE.
DELIVER THE EXPERIENCE.
HOW WE RUN IT
STANDARDS & STAYING SHARP
EDUCATION SCORE & REPORTING
Ultimately owned by the Venue Manager — day-to-day with the TM & assistants (see its own page).
Monitor who needs more training before it shows up on track.
An Education Score of 2 or above across a fortnight → submit a report: why, and what's being done about it. To be corrected by end of the following month.
SPEED 4 — LEADS & MANAGERS
Track Leads, TM & ATM must be Speed 4 qualified prior to being appointed.
Each month: 2 races, 0 spins, 6 laps under the Speed 4 time — submit that report to the VM.
Team Leads: Speed 4 approved within one month of becoming a lead — VM manages this.
One weekend shift a fortnight to keep the role. Away longer than 4 weeks → the role is re-allocated.
STAY SHARP & TALK ABOUT WHAT MATTERS
Race at least once a fortnight — management team keeps skills sharp and checks the track is at the right level, so the management team can discuss it.
Education races, you can drive on shift in quiet times — as long as it's genuinely for education, and noted as such in the computer race notes.

Pizza & Play is your chance to have fun, bond with team and share skills — helping each other as a team matters as much as helping customers. It is so important for the culture of your venue. GMac wants you to share in his passion and enjoy the experience hence he closes the store early once a month so you can all have fun – please respect this opportunity.
The experience — safety, track, karts, education — is the number one thing in every team meeting. Without it, nothing else matters. Get it right and everything else looks after itself.
Be proactive about the experience — nothing else matters.  ·  41 / 51
practicalCriteria: Knows who owns what: VM, TM/ATM, Track Leads
Applies the standards: Speed 4 monthly, race once a fortnight

modules.title: The Track Team — Structure and Reporting
content: ONE TEAM.
ONE MACHINE.
THE TRACK TEAM
STRUCTURE, REPORTING & WHERE THE ACADEMY FITS
Who reports to whom — and where each role sits on the PowerPlay Academy ladder.
VM
VENUE MANAGER   ·   RUNS THE VENUE
Owns the Education Score and the venue. The Track Manager reports here.
TM
TRACK MANAGER   ·   REPORTS TO VENUE MANAGER
The go-to for Leads and attendants — owns the track, the karts and training.
ACADEMY: WRC
ATM
ASSISTANT TRACK MANAGER   ·   REPORTS TO TRACK MANAGER
Head decision-maker and main contact on the Track Manager’s days off.
ACADEMY: WRC
TL
TRACK LEADS   ·   LEAD THE FLOOR
Set the example in front of the customer; coach the coaches.
ACADEMY: RALLY
TA
TRACK ATTENDANTS & JUNIORS   ·   RUN THE RACES
Deliver the experience — briefing to de-brief.
ACADEMY: TRAINEE → ROOKIE
ALONGSIDE THE TRACK TEAM
Customer Experience Squad — front of house: the first hello and the last goodbye. One team with the track.
Kart Maintenance Technician — services and maintains the karts with the TM & ATM.
Escalation — Track Operations Manager / Senior Track Manager (State) → Daymon & GMac.
One team. One machine.  ·  42 / 51
practicalCriteria: Explains the track team structure and reporting lines

modules.title: Track Manager — Role
content: THE GO-TO
FOR THE FLOOR.
TRACK MANAGER
REPORTS TO: VENUE MANAGER  ·  ACADEMY LEVEL: WRC
The go-to person for Track Leads and attendants. Still hands-on — handles attendant and lead duties when needed.
SAFETY & STANDARDS
Safety is the top priority — never compromise on established procedures.
Set and uphold the standard for Leads and attendants.
TRAINING — NEVER-ENDING
Run the training program; there’s always something to improve.
Update the VM on staff progress — it drives rostering.
Spend time educating staff on driving techniques.
THE KARTS
Maintain all electric karts; follow the Kart Maintenance Schedule.
Document every shift on the maintenance sheets — plan part replacement ahead.
THE TRACK
Manage grip levels and speed cutoffs — challenging yet safe.
Resolve track and venue faults; coordinate repairs.
THE SYSTEMS
Clubspeed — purchase orders, stock reports, service notes & part life.
Connecteam — resolve every kart/track fault report; opening & closing checklists.
VENUE PRESENTATION
Driver Zone, Pit Lane and Maintenance Area at PowerPlay standard.
Unsure? Ask: does it make it more fun or more safe? If not, we don’t do it.
More fun or more safe — or we don’t do it.  ·  43 / 51
practicalCriteria: Describes the Track Manager’s responsibilities and ownership

modules.title: Assistant Track Manager — Role
content: SECOND IN
COMMAND.
ASSISTANT TRACK MANAGER
REPORTS TO: TRACK MANAGER  ·  ACADEMY LEVEL: WRC
Works alongside the Track Manager — and is the head decision-maker on the TM’s days off.
LEADING SHIFTS
Head decision-maker and main point of contact on the TM’s days off.
Think ahead — be prepared and aware of what’s coming up.
THE STANDARD
Safety and fun first, for every customer.
Lead the way with Juniors and Leads — that’s how you earn respect.
TRAINING
Lead the training program day-to-day; give feedback.
Use your track knowledge to educate others — customer education and safety.
KARTS & TRACK
Work with the TM and the Kart Maintenance Technician on servicing.
Report kart and track anomalies; follow up until resolved.
THE SYSTEMS
Clubspeed — purchase orders, stock reports, service notes.
Connecteam — follow up every fault report with the TM.
VENUE PRESENTATION
Keep the track, Driver Zone, Pit Lane and Maintenance Area clean.
Opening and closing checklists — follow up the notes.
Ready on the days that matter.  ·  44 / 51
practicalCriteria: Describes the Assistant Track Manager’s responsibilities and support role

modules.title: WRC Certification
content: KNOW IT.
PROVE IT.
LEVEL 4 · WRC
TRACK MANAGER — CERTIFICATION
TYPICAL DURATION:  min. 3 months as a Rally
ONLINE TEST
Everything in Rally, plus all of Part Two
Incidents & faults: report, review, record & close
Education Score: ownership + the >2 / fortnight report
Track & karts: slippery by design; grip tape (TM/ATM/VM only)
Roles & ownership: VM, TM/ATM, Track Leads
Standards: Speed 4 monthly, race once a fortnight
Pass:  100% on safety questions, 90% overall.
PRACTICAL ASSESSMENT
Maintains Speed 4 monthly (2 races, 0 spins, 6 laps under the time)
Runs an incident review and writes an action plan
Manages the Education Score; reports if >2 / fortnight
Develops and signs off Leads
Applies the grip-tape strategy; advises Daymon
Reports monthly: education score, word of mouth, incidents
SIGNED OFF BY:  Senior Track Manager (State) and/or Daymon & GMac
WRC → Champion  ·  49 / 51

onlineTest.passRule: By standards review with Daymon & GMac.

modules.title: Thats The Playbook — Recap
content: THAT'S THE PLAYBOOK.
REMEMBER

We don't sell go-karting.
We sell the feeling.
Educate well and the race runs itself — fewer spins, fewer stoppages, bigger smiles. Every detail matters. No shortcuts. Education is the key. Bigger smiles are the reward.
P
Passionate
R
Respectful
A
Accountable
C
Committed
E
Empowering
Your two priorities, every shift: 1  Keep people safe    2  Deliver the experience
LET'S RACE.
Now go deliver it.
We sell the feeling — and you're the one who creates it.
practicalCriteria: Demonstrates command of the whole playbook, level to level
Can teach any part of it to others

modules.title: Champion Certification
content: KNOW IT.
PROVE IT.
LEVEL 5 · CHAMPION
SENIOR LEADERS — CERTIFICATION
TYPICAL DURATION:  By invitation · proven record
KNOWLEDGE & STANDARDS
Cross-venue standards & auditing the experience
Developing and certifying Track Managers
Education Score trends across venues
Word of mouth and the measures that matter
Living PRACE and the GMac Standard
Pass:  By standards review with Daymon & GMac.
PRACTICAL ASSESSMENT
Proven record: venues consistently deliver the experience
Low Education Scores & strong word of mouth across venues
Develops and certifies Track Managers
Audits and lifts standards across the group
Sets the example others follow
SIGNED OFF BY:  Daymon & GMac only
Champion → GMac Standard  ·  50 / 51

modules.title: The GMac Standard
content: THE GOLD
STANDARD.
THE GMAC STANDARD
THE GOLD STANDARD — DAYMON & GMAC
Not applied for — recognised by Daymon & GMac.
The gold standard of delivering the experience: bonded moments every time, mentors others, lives PRACE, and is the benchmark the whole venue is measured against.
The standard we all chase.  ·  51 / 51
